summary: I served in the Vietnam War from early 1971 to February 1972. It was the latter part of the war, and I spent eleven months flying helicopters in South Vietnam. During my tour, I witnessed major offensives near An Loc, Loc Ninh, and Tay Ninh, including incursions into Cambodia. The war was intense, but I also got to see the beautiful countryside and interact with local people.

I enlisted in the United States Army because I wanted to choose my own military job rather than be drafted. I became an aviator and flew helicopters during my service. I stayed in the Army for almost twenty-five years and nine months, serving in various roles including flying large cargo helicopters after Vietnam. The Army gave me the training and opportunities to develop my skills as a pilot.

Before I enlisted, I had just graduated with my associate's degree from Pennsylvania State University. I knew the draft was looming, so I decided to enlist in the Army rather than wait to be drafted. My education helped me prepare for the challenges ahead and gave me a foundation before entering military service.

I spent nearly a year in South Vietnam, primarily at a place called Plantation near Bien Hoa and Phu Loi. My unit operated throughout the III Corps area, supporting both US and Vietnamese forces. South Vietnam was a diverse and beautiful country with mountains, plantations, rivers, and beaches, which I saw often while flying missions.

Plantation was my base of operations during my entire tour in Vietnam. It was located between Bien Hoa and Phu Loi, near the 93rd Replacement Company. From Plantation, I flew missions all over III Corps, doing combat support, resupply, and medevac. The base had a mess hall and officers' club where we could relax after flying.
cues: between Bien Hoa and Phu Loi

summary: Bien Hoa was one of the more populated areas near where I was stationed. Occasionally, we would go there for some downtime or to enjoy restaurants like the Mandarin House. Bien Hoa was also a place where entertainers like Bob Hope performed for the troops.

Phu Loi was close to Plantation and the 93rd Replacement Company, where new servicemen arrived in southern Vietnam. While I didn’t spend much time there, it was part of the area where I was based during my tour. It was a reference point for the location of my unit’s operations.

The 93rd Replacement Company was the processing point for new servicemen arriving in southern Vietnam. My base at Plantation was within a mile of this company, so it was a familiar landmark. It was where soldiers were assigned to their units after arriving in country.

I was assigned to the 117th Aviation Company during my entire tour in Vietnam. We operated out of Plantation and flew UH-1 Hueys in a Combat Support Unit. Our missions varied widely from combat assaults to medevac and resupply, supporting both US and Vietnamese forces across III Corps.

III Corps was the area of operations where I flew most of my missions. It covered a large region in South Vietnam, including places like Tay Ninh, An Loc, and Loc Ninh. Flying over III Corps allowed me to see diverse terrain and participate in major offensives during my tour.

I received my primary aviation training at Fort Walters, Texas, where I spent about four months. This was the first phase of my helicopter pilot training before moving on to more advanced instruction. It prepared me for flying Hueys in Vietnam.

After Fort Walters, I went to Fort Rucker for the second phase of my helicopter training. There, I learned instrument flying and flight tactics specific to the Huey helicopter. This training was essential for the complex missions I flew in Vietnam, including night flare missions.

I flew the UH-1 Huey helicopter throughout my Vietnam tour with the 117th Aviation Company. The Huey was versatile and used for combat assaults, resupply, medevac, and flare missions. I trained extensively on the Huey at Fort Walters and Fort Rucker before deploying.
cues: training at Fort Walters and Fort Rucker

summary: During my time in Vietnam, I flew missions that took me into Thailand on submissions for the Embassy. It was interesting to fly outside Vietnam and see a different country in the region. These flights were part of the broader scope of my aviation duties.

Song Bay was one of the most beautiful and mountainous areas I flew over in III Corps. It had French rubber plantations and a mountain with radio and intelligence sites, including a landing pad called Strawberry Pad. The local Mountain-Yards lived self-sufficiently in the area, which was fascinating to observe.

During my tour, there were major offensives that extended into Cambodia, especially near Tay Ninh. We discovered large North Vietnamese units infiltrating through Cambodia into III Corps border areas. Our missions included supporting these offensives and dealing with the challenges of cross-border fighting.

An Lộc was one of the key locations in the northwestern part of III Corps where major offensives occurred during my tour. We flew many missions supporting Vietnamese units in that area. It was a hotspot during the later stages of the war.

Loc Ninh was another critical area in III Corps where we supported combat operations. It was part of the region affected by North Vietnamese infiltration and major offensives. Our helicopter missions often involved resupply and combat support in Loc Ninh.

Tây Ninh was a base where we could refuel and resupply during missions. I often flew flare ship missions at night out of Tây Ninh to illuminate enemy positions. It was also near the border with Cambodia and a focal point during the offensives we supported.

Firebase Pace was one of the firebases we supported near Tây Ninh. It was under siege for about fifty days, which made it a notable location during the war. Our unit flew resupply and reconnaissance missions to assist the defenders there.

The Bell AH-1 Cobra was part of the Hunter Killer Teams we flew with during combat missions. Alongside Hueys and OH-6 Cayuse helicopters, the Cobras provided armed escort and fire support. Flying with these teams was an intense and coordinated effort.

The Hughes OH-6 Cayuse was another helicopter in our Hunter Killer Teams. It was used for reconnaissance and scouting missions while we flew combat support. Working alongside Hueys and Cobras, the OH-6 played a vital role in our operations.

During my Vietnam tour, I was awarded twenty-two Air Medals for flying numerous hours in combat and combat support missions. The Air Medal was a recognition of the risks and dedication involved in flying in a war zone. It was a common award among pilots completing their tours.

At the completion of my tour, I received the Bronze Star Medal, which was typical for soldiers finishing their Vietnam service. It recognized my contributions and service during a challenging time. Along with other medals, it marked the end of my combat tour.

I was awarded the Vietnam Campaign Medal as part of my service recognition. It was a standard medal given to those who served in Vietnam during the conflict. It symbolized my participation in the campaign and my time in-country.

Bien Hoa Air Base was a major nearby facility to our base at Plantation. We occasionally went there for rest or to see shows, including a visit by Bob Hope. It was one of the more populated and developed areas close to where I was stationed.

We sometimes went into Saigon during our downtime to enjoy restaurants and experience city life. I particularly liked Oriental and Thai food there, and I learned to use chopsticks at a place called Mandarin House. Saigon was a contrast to the combat zones and offered a chance to relax.

Bob Hope visited Bien Hoa during my tour, bringing entertainment to the troops. His presence was a memorable event and boosted morale. It was one of the few notable celebrity visits I experienced while in Vietnam.

We had access to MARS, which allowed us to make phone calls back home, usually about once a month. Communication was mostly through letters, but MARS provided an important way to connect with family. It was a valuable resource during long deployments.

After Vietnam, I was stationed at Fort Eustis, Virginia, where I flew the Sikorsky Skycrane cargo helicopter. This was a different role from flying Hueys in combat, focusing on large cargo transport. Fort Eustis was part of my continued Army aviation career after the war.

The Sikorsky Skycrane was a large cargo helicopter I flew after returning from Vietnam. I did not fly this aircraft in combat but operated it at Fort Eustis. It was a significant change from the Huey, focusing on heavy lift and transport missions.

I was flying near Cu Chi when I experienced an engine failure in my Huey. We performed an autorotation landing without injury or damage. Cu Chi was part of the area where I flew many missions and encountered challenges.
Bà Tri was near where I had an engine failure while flying a Huey. After the emergency landing, we formed a perimeter and called for help. It was part of the operational area during my Vietnam tour.

During my service, I held the rank of Chief Warrant Officer 2. This rank reflected my role as a helicopter pilot and my experience in the Army. It was a position of responsibility within my aviation unit.
